articles.headline: Seymour Cunningham -- a fugitive's self-mutilation to match his brother's freedom certificate
articles.summary: Reprinted account (via the New-England Galaxy / Old Hampshire Courier) of Seymour Cunningham, a mulatto apprehended in the streets of Boston as a runaway slave from Alexandria and examined before Justice Whitman. He produced his brother's freedom certificate; the agent conceded it was genuine but showed it described war wounds (lost finger and toe, fractured leg, shot-away calf, broken arm) the prisoner lacked. The people of colour in Boston held a caucus to protect him, and Cunningham -- declaring 'liberty is sweet' -- had a butcher sworn to secrecy mutilate him to conform to the certificate (biting off his own middle finger and striking off his own toe first). Named participants in the caucus scene include Augustus Howard (who held the $50 raised 'for the Greeks'), 'old Congo,' and Cato York. At a second trial he was nevertheless delivered to the agent and returned to slavery in Alexandria, then restored to liberty at the price of six hundred dollars subscribed by the coloured people of Boston or procured by them from the friends of emancipation.
articles.notes: The narrative is presented as fact from the prior winter; the story's grim satire ('Hail, Columbia!') is preserved in the reprint. Names within the dramatized caucus may be part-literary.

articles.headline: Views of the Benevolent Society of Alexandria, No. I
articles.summary: First number of a serialized exposition (from the Alexandria Gazette) of the Benevolent Society of Alexandria for ameliorating and improving the condition of the People of Colour. It announces the society's plan to lay before the public a candid exposition of its sentiments: that the domestic slave trade in the District of Columbia is an evil of serious magnitude carried on 'with circumstances of the most afflicting and heart-rending cruelty -- separating husband and wife, parents and children'; that the society intends no interference with the constitutional rights of slave-holders but will document the evil's extent and promote gradual manumission and colonization. It surveys anti-slavery progress in Great Britain, South American republics, a new Maryland society, and roughly fifty societies in North Carolina. The society's constitution could be seen at the Freedom's Journal office.
articles.notes: Series continues as No. II (comparative cost of free and slave labour, FJ_1827-06-01), No. III (why slave labour is dearer, FJ_1827-06-08), and No. IV (political tendency of slavery, FJ_1827-06-15, closing with a long extract from Jefferson's Notes on Virginia).

articles.headline: Fuel Savings Fund -- new society organized in New-York
articles.summary: Editorial notice that a Fuel Savings Fund society has been organized in New York City under the direction and patronage of many of the most respectable and wealthy citizens, with two objects: assistance of the afflicted poor without distinction of nation, colour, or religion, and laying in fuel in summer at cheap rates to sell at cost in winter. Deposits of two shillings or more would be received from labouring citizens at the office in the New-York Institution (opposite the Savings Bank, Chamber-street) on Saturday evenings in June, entitling depositors to purchase fuel at summer cost from December 15 to March 15. The editors strongly recommend the institution to the coloured brethren of the city as an exercise of economy: 'a shilling saved is as good as two shillings gained.'

articles.headline: Summary -- Pennsylvania Conference of the African Methodist Episcopal Church in session in Philadelphia
articles.summary: Among the week's summary items: 'The Pennsylvania Conference of the African Methodist E. Church are now holding their annual session in Philadelphia.' The same column notes the Annual Conference of the Zion Connexion in session in New York, the N.Y. (Methodist Episcopal) Conference in Troy, the people of colour in Belmont county, Ohio recently establishing a regular school for the education of their children, and a shocking murder committed in April on the body of a free woman of colour residing near Charlottesville, Va.
articles.notes: Short but valuable: places the AME annual conference in Philadelphia in late May 1827.

articles.headline: Proposal for a temperance society among the people of colour, to be formed on Emancipation Day
articles.author: R (correspondent)
articles.summary: A correspondent signing 'R' urges the people of colour to follow the example of white citizens who have formed societies for the suppression of intemperance, and to organize such a society among themselves on July 4, 1827 -- the day slavery is abolished throughout New York State. He argues the coloured people may be ranked among the most temperate classes (having attended hundreds of entertainments where liquors were served and never seen more than three persons the worse for drinking), but that intemperance is more hurtful to them than to others: debarred from many livelihoods and labouring under strong prejudice, 'our very existence therefore depends upon temperance.' Every coloured man in the place is urged to become a member, 'distinguishing the day in a most honourable and useful manner.'
articles.notes: The editors second the proposal in the same issue's editorial column: no time more suitable than 'that glorious day, which gives liberty and all its blessings, to a portion of our brethren.'

articles.headline: African Free Schools in the United States -- a city-by-city census of coloured schooling
articles.summary: Major editorial surveying schools for coloured children in the northern cities: Portland, Me. (coloured population 900; one school under a mistress); Boston (2,000; three schools -- two primary under African female teachers and a grammar school under a master -- assisted by the late Abiel Smith's bequest, itemized in a note: $4,000 of three per cent stock plus turnpike, bridge and bathing-house shares); Salem (400; a school opened last year but closed after six months); New-Haven (800; two schools three months in the year); Providence, R.I. (1,500) and Hartford, Conn. (500) provide none; PHILADELPHIA (20,000; three schools under four teachers); New-York (15,000; two schools under a master and mistress, able parents paying $1 per quarter per child). The editors argue the scarcity explains any apparent backwardness -- 'let the most intelligent people upon earth be enslaved for ages... and we venture to assert, that we should behold beings as ignorant, degraded' -- attack incompetent teachers foisted on coloured schools, invoke South America and Hayti where the man of colour 'acts and feels himself a man,' and call on friends of humanity everywhere to establish schools 'for our too-long neglected people.'
articles.notes: The Philadelphia figures (20,000 coloured population; 3 schools, 4 teachers) are a key comparative datum for the 1838 Black Metropolis project.

articles.headline: I Wish I Was a Man
articles.author: NED (pseudonym)
articles.summary: Original moral tale for the Freedom's Journal: little Harry Morton wishes away his boyhood to be a man, and the narrator, returning after long absence, finds him aged, widowed, bereft of his three children, 'a sadder but a wiser man' -- a lesson to the young to be contented with their lot and enjoy the season of youth.

articles.headline: Reply to the Princeton N.J. Patriot on colonization
articles.author: A Man of Colour (pseudonym)
articles.summary: A correspondent answers strictures in the New-Jersey Patriot of May 24 against his earlier anti-colonization piece. He notes his sentiments are his own, not the editors'; defends his quotations from Henry Clay's speech as correct, including Clay's assertion that the Colonization Society has nothing to do with the delicate question of slavery but is to be exclusively applied to the free people; and asks why, if these are not the Society's views, no member contradicted them. If true, 'the Man of Colour has cause to feel alarm at the progress of a Society whose object is ultimately to get rid of the free population.' He rejects the Patriot's threat that the Journal will lose all its patrons among the friends of colonization.

articles.headline: Kosciusko School
articles.author: AMICUS (pseudonym)
articles.summary: First of two communications urging the people of colour to realize General Kosciusko's testamentary bequest for the education of coloured youth ('the Kosciusko School'). Education is 'the great cause of man'; the writer fears 'we shall be defrauded of the money, through a flaw in the instrument; but let not the WILL, which was in him, be wanting in us, for whom he so nobly willed' -- with the will they will find the way. A second number (FJ_1827-06-15, p.3) continues: the school 'exists in the sacred Will and there it cannot be broken up,' and the free people of colour in America stand 'at the head of this great nation' with its greatest resources now in their own hands.
articles.notes: Refers to Thaddeus Kosciuszko's American will leaving his U.S. assets to free and educate enslaved people, then tied up in litigation.

articles.headline: Colonization Society -- reply to the Georgetown Columbian and District Advertiser
articles.summary: Extended editorial answering the Georgetown Columbian and District Advertiser of May 29, 1827, which had sneered that the Journal was run by 'free negroes... or rather, we fear, busy white men,' accused it of prejudicing its brethren against the Colonization Society, and declared that 'Nature never intended a black and white population to reside among one another... unless one be totally subject to the other.' The editors reply that no gentleman of education acquainted with them has ever doubted their competency; deny making any effort to prejudice their brethren against the Society or that they oppose colonization in principle (having presented themselves as open to conviction, viewing it favourably 'merely considered as a missionary establishment'); assert the equal rights of the people of colour to this country -- 'the time has come in which we cannot submit to any compromise whatever'; argue a missionary family on Dr. Carey's plan would effect more in ten years for Africa than the colony in twenty; question whether the colony has prevented or merely diverted the exportation of slaves; and demand a calm and thorough discussion of a subject 'in which the man of colour is so deeply interested' before another step is taken -- until convinced, their duty is to say to their brethren, 'Abide in the ship, or you cannot be saved.'
articles.notes: One of the paper's fullest early statements on colonization; the 'Abide in the ship' close became a touchstone.

articles.headline: Death in Arch-street Prison of William Pettigon alias Bill Paragee, kidnapper of Philadelphia's black children
articles.summary: From a Philadelphia paper (P. A. D. Adv.): William Pettigon, alias Bill Paragee, a white man of Nanticoke river, arrested about a month before on board the pilot boat William Price and committed for trial before the next Circuit Court of the United States, charged with being the captain of Joe Johnson's sloop Little John and an active conspirator in the kidnapping of a number of black children from Philadelphia in the summer of 1825, died in Arch-street Prison, 'thereby escaping the almost certainty of a capital conviction.' On examination before the Mayor the proof against him was strong and decisive, much of it voluntarily admitted. Henry Carr, a black man said to be one of Johnson's agents, remained in prison awaiting trial before the next Mayor's Court.
articles.notes: Direct documentation of the Cannon-Johnson kidnapping ring's Philadelphia operations; pairs with the John Purnell arrest item in the same issue.

articles.headline: John Purnell arrested in Boston for stealing free coloured children from Philadelphia
articles.summary: 'A man of colour, calling himself John Purnell, from Snow-hill, Md., has been arrested in Boston, charged by the proclamation of the Mayor of Philadelphia with stealing free coloured children from that city and selling them for slaves.'
articles.notes: Purnell was a notorious agent of the Cannon-Johnson gang; the Mayor of Philadelphia's proclamation and reward led to this arrest.

articles.headline: Death of Dr. Peaco, U.S. Agent, at Savannah; ship Norfolk to carry recaptured Africans to Monrovia
articles.summary: Dr. Peaco, U.S. Agent, died at Savannah on May 23 of an inflammatory fever contracted on his voyage from Norfolk. He had arrived the previous evening in the ship Norfolk, on her way to Monrovia, sent to transport to Africa under his direction about 130 of the Africans carried into Savannah in the slave ship General Ramirez (captured in 1820 by the revenue cutter Crawford, Capt. Jackson), at the expense of the government of Spain; about 40 more remained at the expense of the U.S. Government.

articles.headline: St. Augustine Catholic Church, Philadelphia, robbed
articles.summary: Summary item: on a Saturday evening St. Augustine (Catholic) Church in Philadelphia was entered and robbed of silver vessels to a considerable amount.

articles.headline: Hayti, No. IV -- from the Scrap-Book of Africanus
articles.author: Africanus (pseudonym)
articles.summary: Fourth number of the serialized history of Hayti: the renewed revolt of the people of colour in the Western Division; the concordat of September 11, 1791 admitting the national decree of May 15; the National Assembly's fatal repeal of that decree (September 24, 1791) which annulled the concordat and drove the people of colour again to arms; the reinstating decree of April 4, 1792 and the commissioners Santhonax, Polverel and Ailhaud; the conflict between the commissioners and governor Galbaud; the entry of five thousand revolters into Cape Haytien on June 24, 1793, and the commissioners' proclamation abolishing slavery throughout St. Domingo, ratified by the National Assembly February 5, 1794; the flight of colonists (a large fleet arriving at Norfolk, Virginia); Napoleon's 1802 expedition under Le Clerc with 40,000 men; the treacherous midnight seizure of Toussaint and his family; the war of extermination with bloodhounds from Cuba; Rochambeau's capitulation to the Haytiens and English blockade; and Dessalines proclaiming independence January 1, 1804.
articles.notes: Continues the series begun in earlier numbers; a principal early Black-press narrative of the Haitian Revolution.

articles.headline: Manumission Societies -- the man of colour's best friends
articles.summary: Editorial (companion to the previous week's colonization reply) defending the purity of the Abolitionists' motives: 'No man of colour, of common sense... has ever doubted the purity of their motives. Their uniform conduct has ever proved them the man of colour's best friends.' The editors credit abolition societies with instituting the present free schools, marvel at the change wrought in public opinion in forty years, chide brethren who neglect their good counsels, and call on each to divest himself of 'Self' so that friends may 'behold something more pleasing to them, and creditable to us.'

articles.headline: Coloured boy killed in five-story fall at Seventh and Mulberry streets, Philadelphia
articles.summary: From the Philadelphia Gazette: about two o'clock in the morning a coloured boy aged about eight, who was sick and had been entrusted to the care of his parents -- servants of the family at the southwest corner of Seventh and Mulberry streets -- fell from the attic into the kitchen area, a distance of five stories, and survived only a few hours. His father had administered medicine to him after midnight; it is supposed the confused child mistook the window for the door while seeking his parents. The same issue's summary also notes a boy about twelve run over and shockingly mangled by a dray on the Philadelphia wharves below Spruce-street, and that the two coloured men charged by Rice, the robber of the Ohio treasury, with being his accomplices had been discharged.
articles.notes: Mulberry street is present-day Arch street; the item documents a live-in Black servant family in 1827 Philadelphia.

events.name: Pennsylvania Annual Conference of the African Methodist Episcopal Church, 1827
events.description: The Pennsylvania Conference of the African Methodist Episcopal Church held its annual session in Philadelphia in late May 1827, as reported in the Freedom's Journal summary column of May 25 ('are now holding their annual session in Philadelphia').

events.name: Annual Conference of the A.M.E. Zion Connexion, New York, 1827
events.description: The Annual Conference of the Zion Connexion (A.M.E. Zion) was in session in New York City in late May 1827, per the same summary column.

events.name: Marriage of Adam Brown and Elizabeth Peterson
events.description: Married on Sunday evening May 27, 1827 by the Rev. Mr. [Peter] Williams: Mr. Adam Brown of Bergen, N.J. to Mrs. Elizabeth Peterson of New York. The same notice records that 'last evening, by the same,' Mr. James Fraser of the Island of Barbadoes was married to Miss Anne Miles of Charleston, S.C. (May 31, 1827).

events.name: Marriage of Charles Chandler and Mary Brodgart
events.description: Married on the 6th inst. by the Rev. B[enjamin] Paul: Mr. Charles Chandler to Miss Mary Brodgart. The same column's death notices include: in Philadelphia, on the 20th ult. (May 20, 1827), Mrs. Rosannah Chapman, aged 34; in New York, Mrs. Judith Brown, aged 40, and Mrs. Phebe Cross, aged one hundred and six.
